segments.caption: The video opens with a futuristic digital animation of a starfield and data streams, transitioning into a high-speed journey through a digital tunnel with the text 'i am a visionary' appearing prominently. This shifts to a detailed astronomical image of the spiral galaxy NGC 628, labeled with scientific credits and highlighting 'Dust Enshrouded Stellar Nurseries' in vibrant orange and blue hues. The scene then transitions to a satellite view of Taiwan, overlaid with atmospheric data from NVIDIA CorrDiff, showing a dramatic, swirling storm in vivid purples and oranges. The narrative continues with a digital Earth rotating, accompanied by the text 'i am a helper,' before cutting to a man wearing a dotLumen head-mounted device walking on a city street, with a green digital grid projected onto the pavement. The video then shows a woman in a wheelchair, connected to brain-computer interface equipment, with a screen displaying her face and real-time neural data, including phonetic breakdowns of speech. The final scene features an industrial facility with conveyor belts and machinery, overlaid with the text 'i am a transformer,' suggesting technological transformation and innovation.

segments.caption: The video opens with a Hippocratic AI User, a woman in a modern living room, sitting on a couch and holding a tablet displaying a healthcare professional’s face, suggesting a remote medical consultation. The scene transitions to a futuristic digital tunnel with glowing lines and text, where the phrase 'i am a navigator' appears, followed by a prompt input interface typing 'build busy metropolitan streets.' This leads to a montage of simulated urban driving scenarios from a vehicle’s perspective, showcasing AI-driven navigation through city traffic, including interactions with red double-decker buses and pedestrians. The AI system, labeled 'Wayve AI Driver,' responds to queries like 'Tell me what you are doing,' with real-time explanations such as 'I am overtaking a stopped bus from the right.' The narrative then shifts to a text generation interface from AI21 Labs, where a prompt asks for rephrasing of 'I bring the words to life, regardless of the language,' generating poetic variations. This transitions into a deepdub interface, showing waveform editing with multilingual text overlays, including 'and I wrote the music' and 'y escribí la música.' The final segment features a live orchestral performance by the London Symphony Orchestra, with musicians playing violins, trombones, and other instruments under a conductor’s direction, with on-screen text crediting AIIVA for original music composition and John Paesano for arrangement. The video concludes with a return to the digital tunnel, reinforcing the theme of AI as a transformative force.

segments.caption: The video segment opens with a dynamic ocean wave simulation rendered in real-time, showcasing the power of NVIDIA Warp, with a distant island visible on the horizon under a bright sky. The scene transitions to a 3D rendering environment displaying a collection of colorful, reflective spheres and a pile of dark granular material, labeled with OpenPBR and PhysX, indicating advanced material and physics simulation. Next, a detailed 3D model of a knight statue is shown disintegrating into fragments, demonstrating PhysX Flow’s destruction simulation capabilities. This is followed by a dramatic fire simulation engulfing a room, with flames dynamically interacting with the environment, highlighting PhysX Flow’s realistic fire effects. The video then shifts to a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation of airflow around a car, using Siemens STARCCM+ with Omniverse Cloud APIs, visualized with vibrant color gradients. A digital twin of an industrial facility is shown, with real-time data overlays and 3D models of machinery, powered by Cadence Reality Digital Twin Platform and other tools. The segment continues with a close-up of a textured, metallic object, possibly a part of a robot or machinery, rendered with high realism using Warp. A code editor is shown with a terminal command 'ov-code-gen --prompt="simulate robot gripper"', indicating AI-driven code generation for simulation tasks. The scene transitions to a 3D simulation of a robotic arm in a factory setting, interacting with objects on a conveyor belt, labeled with Isaac Sim, PowerBI, and Rockwell Automation Emulate3D. A robotic arm is then shown in a kitchen environment, placing a pan into an oven, demonstrating Amazon Robotics’ capabilities. The video then displays a simulation of multiple humanoid robots navigating a complex, obstacle-filled environment, labeled with Isaac Lab, Agility Robotics, Apptronik, and Unitree. Finally, the segment concludes with a warehouse simulation where AI-driven tools like NVIDIA NIM - DeepSearch and SimReady USD are used to manage and organize 3D models of barrels, pallets, and forklifts, showcasing automated scene generation and object placement in a logistics environment.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a technical comparison slide titled 'BLACKWELL VERSUS HOPPER,' highlighting a massive leap in compute with twice the size, 128 billion more transistors, 5x AI performance, and 4x on-die memory, displayed against a black background with a detailed chip diagram. The scene transitions to a close-up of the BLACKWELL chip, described as 'THE ENGINE OF THE NEW INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION,' showcasing 20 petaFLOPS of AI performance, 192GB of HBM3e, 8TB/s memory bandwidth, and full CUDA stack support. Next, the video illustrates the integration of two BLACKWELL GPUs and one GRACE CPU, forming the building blocks of the GB200 superchip, with 384GB of HBM3e, 72 Arm Neoverse V2 CPU cores, and 900GB/s NVLink-C2C bandwidth. The GB200 GRACE BLACKWELL SUPERCHIP is then shown as a unified processor for trillion-parameter-scale generative AI, featuring 40 petaFLOPS, 864GB fast memory, 16TB/s HBM, and 3.6TB/s NVLink bandwidth. The BLACKWELL COMPUTE NODE is introduced as the most powerful compute node ever created, with two Grace CPUs and four Blackwell GPUs, delivering 80 petaFLOPS, 1.7TB HBM3e, 32TB/s memory bandwidth, and a liquid-cooled MGX design. The video then reveals the CONNECTX-800G INFINIBAND SUPERNIC, optimized for AI processing with advanced GPU RDMA, adaptive routing, and programmable congestion control. Following this, the BLUEFIELD-3 DPU is shown as a powerful infrastructure processor with line-speed networking, storage, and cybersecurity processing, and 80GB/s memory bandwidth. Finally, the GB200 NVL72 is presented as compute for the modern data center, featuring 18 compute trays in a rack, 36 Grace CPUs, and 72 Blackwell GPUs, all displayed with sleek, modular hardware designs and technical specifications on a black background. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' is consistently displayed in the top right corner, and the 'y highlights' logo appears in the bottom right throughout the segment.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a focus on the GB200 NVL72, a high-performance compute system for modern data centers, shown as a stacked rack with 18 compute trays, 36 Grace CPUs, and 72 Blackwell GPUs. The scene transitions to a detailed view of the NVLink Switch, highlighting two NVLink Switch chips with eight ports at 1.8TB/s, aggregating to 14.4TB/s bandwidth, connected via light blue cables. This evolves into the NVLink Switch System, featuring 18 NVLink Switch chips and 72 ports, enabling all-to-all communications at 130TB/s. The narrative then integrates the GB200 Compute Nodes, NVLink Switch, and Spine into a unified system, described as 'one massive GPU for generative AI,' with 72 Blackwell GPUs fully connected by NVLink, using cost-effective copper cabling and blind-mate connectors for easy serviceability. The GB200 NVL72 is further detailed as delivering 1.4 exaFLOPS of AI performance, 30TB of HBM3e, and functioning as one giant CUDA GPU. The Quantum InfiniBand Switch is introduced, offering 800Gb/s per port and 230.4Tb/s aggregated bidirectional throughput. The segment expands to show multiple Quantum-X800 InfiniBand or Spectrum-X800 Ethernet switches, forming a network for the highest AI performance with 14.4 teraFLOPS of in-network computing. The final frames reveal a full-scale deployment of eight GB200 NVL72 compute racks, totaling 288 Grace CPUs and 576 Blackwell GPUs, with 2X reduced rack cooling power, connected via a liquid-cooling system. The entire setup is shown scaling into a vast data center environment, emphasizing massive, interconnected compute power for generative AI. Throughout, the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y highlights' logo remain in the top right and bottom right corners, respectively, against a black background, maintaining a sleek, technical aesthetic.

segments.caption: The video opens with a sweeping 3D animation of a massive data center filled with rows of server racks, each labeled with the NVIDIA logo, emphasizing scale and power. segments.caption: The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket over a black shirt, standing on a dark, minimalist stage with focused lighting, holding a small black remote. He gestures expressively as he speaks, introducing the NVIDIA Blackwell platform for trillion-parameter scale generative AI. The screen behind him transitions to a large technical diagram labeled 'ANNOUNCING NVIDIA BLACKWELL PLATFORM FOR TRILLION-PARAMETER SCALE GENERATIVE AI,' featuring a 3D model of a circuit board with two large square components and a central chip, alongside icons and text highlighting key features: AI Superchip (208B Transistors), 2nd Gen Transformer Engine (FP4/FP6 Tensor Core), 5th Generation NVLink (Scales to 576 GPUs), RAS Engine (100% In-System Self-Test), Secure AI (Full Performance Encryption & TEE), and Decompression Engine (800 GB/sec). Jensen Huang walks across the stage, pointing to the screen as he explains. The scene then cuts to a close-up of Jensen Huang, who continues his presentation with animated hand gestures. The screen changes again to a detailed performance comparison slide titled 'Blackwell GPU,' listing specifications such as FP8 (20 PFLOPS, 2.5X Hopper), NEW FP6 (20 PFLOPS, 2.5X), NEW FP4 (40 PFLOPS, 5X), HBM Model Size (740B param, 6X), HBM Bandwidth (34T param/sec, 5X), and NVLINK All-Reduce with SHARP (7.2 TB/s, 4X), all displayed next to the same circuit board image. The on-screen text 'MARCH 18, 2024' remains in the top right, and the 'y/ highlights' logo is visible in the bottom right throughout.

segments.caption: The video segment opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang standing on a dark stage, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote. He gestures toward a large screen displaying a performance graph titled 'GPT-MoE 1.8T Inference (seqlen=32k/1k, FTL=5s)'. The graph plots 'Throughput per GPU Tokens per Second' on the y-axis against 'Interactivity per User Tokens per Second' on the x-axis, with blue data points forming a dense cluster and a green line labeled 'GB200 FP4' tracing an optimal performance curve. The graph highlights three key configurations: 'TP2.EP8.DP4', 'TP2.EP16.PP2', and 'TP4.EP16', with the green line indicating the superior performance of the GB200 FP4 setup. In the top right corner, a box lists 'Multi-Dimensional Optimization: Tensor Parallel, Pipeline Parallel, Expert Parallel, Data Parallel'. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are visible in the top right and bottom right corners, respectively. Jensen Huang moves across the stage, pointing to different parts of the graph, emphasizing the trade-off between throughput and interactivity, and explaining how the GB200 system achieves high performance across various parallelization strategies. The lighting remains focused on him and the screen, maintaining a high-contrast, professional presentation atmosphere.

segments.caption: The video opens with a wide-angle view of a bustling warehouse on March 18, 2024, where workers in safety gear move among stacks of boxes and pallets, while forklifts navigate the aisles. The scene transitions to a multi-camera tracking interface, displaying a grid of live feeds from various angles across the warehouse, alongside a top-down digital map that tracks 30 individuals with color-coded dots and numbers, illustrating real-time movement and spatial awareness. The perspective then shifts to an overhead simulation view, overlaying a green network of interconnected nodes and paths across the warehouse floor, representing an AI-driven logistics optimization system. This network dynamically adjusts as workers and forklifts move, with red lines indicating potential conflicts or blocked routes. The camera zooms into specific aisles, highlighting a forklift navigating a green-lit path, while a worker in a yellow hard hat and blue vest walks alongside. A pop-up interface appears, labeled 'Agent Response,' querying if an unusual situation occurred in aisle 3. The system responds with a text alert: 'A shelf collapsed and the boxes fell from shelving at 3:30 PM, leaving boxes blocking the aisle,' accompanied by a live video feed from Camera_048 showing the incident with green bounding boxes around the fallen boxes. The environment is brightly lit with industrial lighting, and the floor is marked with yellow and green lines for navigation. The 'y/ highlights' logo remains in the bottom right corner throughout, and the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' is displayed in the top right.

segments.caption: The video begins with a close-up of a Siemens Teamcenter X interface, displaying a detailed 3D model of a coffee machine's internal components, including water pumps and heating systems, with a user navigating through the design tree. The scene transitions to a woman in a blue lab coat using a laptop to view a 3D model of a coffee machine, followed by a man in a white shirt and tie collaborating with her, both focused on a screen showing a green spherical structure with windows, likely a conceptual design. The focus then shifts to a black screen with the NVIDIA Omniverse logo, followed by a diagram illustrating the integration of Siemens Xcelerator, RTX Render, and NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud, emphasizing USD data flow. The narrative progresses to a real-world shipyard with large cranes and a massive ship under construction, branded 'HD Hyundai,' transitioning into a highly detailed 3D simulation of the same ship within Siemens Teamcenter X, showcasing its intricate internal systems and exterior. The video then displays a diagram of a user inputting a prompt ('atlantic ocean waves, remote, photography, ocean, sea, morning sky') into Siemens Xcelerator, which connects to RTX Render and NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud via Edify API, resulting in a photorealistic rendering of the ship in a maritime environment. The final frames show the ship's propeller and rudder in the simulation, with a worker in a hard hat observing the digital twin, highlighting the seamless integration of real-world engineering with digital simulation for maritime design and visualization.

segments.caption: The video segment presents a dynamic showcase of advanced 3D customization and rendering technologies across various industries, primarily focusing on automotive, fashion, and consumer goods. It begins with a close-up of a beige puffer jacket from Brickland, featuring a digital overlay of a black branch and numerical data, transitioning into a red sports car driving through a mountainous landscape. The scene shifts to a colorful, multi-hued SUV being customized in a 3D interface, followed by a white handbag with a gold triangular clasp from Configit, where a user interface displays incompatible options for leather color and backdrop. The video returns to the Brickland jacket, now displayed in multiple color variations, before showcasing a sleek black Lotus Eletre SUV, which changes color to green and then to a dark gray, with a small animated character in a racing suit appearing in the corner. The segment continues with a L'Oréal Revitalift Filler serum bottle, where a text box prompts for an 'Abstract background of geometric crystals in diffused magenta and violet hues,' followed by a hand soap bottle with animated lilies and daisies. An Adidas sneaker with a Chicago skyline design is briefly shown, then a silver Hyundai car is customized in a 3D environment with a prompt for a 'Breathtaking swiss mountain landscape.' The final scene features a Land Rover Defender in a snowy mountain environment, with a text overlay reading 'Let’s see it handle extreme conditions,' emphasizing the vehicle's rugged capabilities. Throughout, the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are consistently displayed in the top right and bottom right corners, respectively, indicating a curated highlight reel of digital design and simulation advancements.

segments.caption: The video segment opens with a real-world robotic arm, labeled 'Isaac Manipulator,' performing precise tasks on a black table, including lifting a wooden block and interacting with a mug. The scene transitions to a simulated environment, 'Isaac Sim in Omniverse Digital Twin,' where a robotic arm sorts colored blocks on a checkered floor, demonstrating AI-driven object categorization. The narrative then shifts to a split-screen view: on the left, a human hand demonstrates a task, while on the right, a robot mimics the action, labeled 'DexPilot,' showcasing imitation learning. This is followed by a simulation of multiple robotic arms in a grid-like factory setup, labeled 'Leonardo,' performing coordinated tasks. The video then presents a split-screen of a surgical robot and a digital twin of a human organ, labeled 'Clara Holoscan,' highlighting medical robotics. Next, a warehouse simulation, 'Isaac Perceptor,' shows a robot navigating aisles with thermal overlays, while a real-world robot follows a similar path. The segment transitions to autonomous vehicle simulation, 'DRIVE Sim in Omniverse Digital Twin,' with a split view of sensor data and a realistic city street, followed by a first-person driving perspective with AI-generated navigation paths. The video then shows a tractor in a field, labeled 'ElectriCSheep,' and a simulation of a robotic arm in a factory, labeled 'Pegatron,' performing assembly tasks. A split-screen of a robotic arm and a surgeon, labeled 'Orbita for Surgery,' demonstrates surgical precision. The final scenes include a top-down view of a robotic arm sorting produce, labeled 'Covariant OFER,' and a large-scale warehouse simulation, 'Amazon Robotics,' with blue robotic carts moving boxes through an automated facility, emphasizing industrial automation and digital twin technology.

segments.caption: The video opens with a high-angle shot of a warehouse simulation labeled 'Omniverse Digital Twin' and 'Amazon Robotics,' showing blue robotic carts moving boxes through an automated facility. The scene transitions to a lab where a woman in a gray shirt interacts with a white humanoid robot, waving her hand as the robot mimics the gesture, with a large screen displaying a 3D simulation in the background. Next, a wide shot reveals a spacious lab with multiple humanoid robots—some teal and black, others white and gray—navigating an obstacle course while engineers observe. The focus shifts to a kitchen setting where a white humanoid robot with black gloves holds a laptop, labeled 'Apptronik,' and then to a woman in a teal shirt guiding a teal robot with a tablet, labeled 'Agility Robotics.' A diagram appears, illustrating 'NVIDIA Project GROOT' processing task prompts from language, videos, and demonstrations to generate actions for a humanoid robot, with feedback from observations. This is followed by a simulation in 'Isaac Lab in Omniverse Digital Twin,' showing numerous robots training on a complex terrain with increasing iterations (500, 1000, 3000). Another diagram details the data pipeline from NVIDIA Isaac Sim, text, video, and real robot data through NVIDIA OSMO and heterogeneous compute to Project GROOT and the humanoid robot, with a breakdown of NVIDIA DGX Train and OVX Simulate. The video then shows a split-screen comparison of a simulated and real kitchen environment, both featuring a teal robot performing tasks. Finally, a man wearing a VR headset is shown in a lab, interacting with a robot that places a mug on a table, while others observe in the background.

segments.caption: The video segment opens with a split-screen comparison of a humanoid robot performing a task in a kitchen, labeled 'Teleoperated' on the left and 'Autonomous' on the right, showcasing its ability to pour liquid from a bottle into a glass. The scene transitions to a demonstration of a robot playing a red drum set, mirroring a human drummer on the left, with the label 'Fourier Intelligence' indicating the technology provider. Next, a woman performs a dance move in front of a green wall, with her body movements tracked by a digital skeleton overlay, while a humanoid robot on the right mimics her actions in real-time. This is followed by a three-panel view: the woman on the left, a digital twin of the robot in the center labeled 'Isaac Lab in Omniverse Digital Twin,' and the physical robot on the right, demonstrating seamless motion transfer. A technical diagram then illustrates the NVIDIA Project GROOT workflow, where a user's voice is converted to text via NVIDIA Tokkio, processed by Project GROOT, and translated into robot actions with audio feedback.
